_comment: Per-chart narratives. CI (build_prompt.py -> GitHub Models -> write_narrative.py) overwrites these from live numbers; this committed copy is the fallback so the page always reads well. source = 'fallback' | 'github-models'.

notes.shape: The yield curve graphs US Treasury yields across maturities from 1 month to 30 years, with a normal steep upward slope reflecting higher long-term yields. This slope typically indicates confidence in future economic growth and inflation. An inverted yield curve, where short-term yields exceed long-term yields, has historically preceded recessions by 6-18 months. Currently, the 10-year Treasury yield is 4.54% (Jun 2026), and the 10Y-2Y spread is positive at 0.35 points, indicating the curve has re-steepened above zero after being inverted from 2022 to 2024, unlike its deepest historical inversion at 0.0 points.
notes.signal: The 2s10s spread, the difference between 10-year and 2-year Treasury yields, serves as a key recession indicator. A negative spread (inversion) has preceded all modern US recessions, signaling investor expectations of slowing growth. The curve was inverted from 2022 through 2024 but has since moved positive by 0.35 points, suggesting a partial normalization. Despite this, the NY Fed recession probability remains elevated at 31%, though it is important to note the yield curve often un-inverts shortly before a recession actually materializes, making timing uncertain.
notes.now: The current positive 2s10s spread alongside a 10-year yield of 4.54% suggests a reduced immediacy of recession signals compared to the prior inversion period. However, the relatively high NY Fed recession probability indicates that risks remain significant. The yield curve is signaling caution but not an imminent downturn, reflecting a complex economic outlook.
